---
title: "Model Performance"
---

This page evaluates how well the model predicts SSC (suspended sediment concentration — the mass of particles in a water sample, in mg/L) across 76 holdout sites that were never used during training. "Holdout" means these sites are a genuinely independent test: if the model performs well here, it should generalize to new sites in the real world. Metrics are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. R² (also called NSE, Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency) measures how much of the variation in SSC the model explains: 1.0 is perfect, 0.0 means the model is no better than predicting the long-run average, and negative values mean the model is actively worse than that baseline.

## Observed vs Predicted

Every individual SSC reading from the 76 holdout sites is plotted here — what the model predicted vs. what was actually measured in the lab. The axes are on a log scale because SSC spans several orders of magnitude (from ~1 mg/L in clear streams to >10,000 mg/L in flashy storm events). Points near the 1:1 line (solid diagonal) are accurate; the dashed lines mark the factor-of-two envelope, meaning predictions within 2× of the true value. Scatter above the line means overprediction; below means underprediction. Look for systematic patterns by concentration range — they reveal where the model struggles physically.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-obs-pred
from pathlib import Path
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, ".")
from _scripts.figures import make_obs_vs_pred

fig = make_obs_vs_pred(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## SSC Distribution: Observed vs Predicted

A model can predict individual readings well on average but still systematically miss the tails — under-representing extreme floods or over-representing clear-water conditions. This figure compares the full statistical distribution (histogram or density curve) of observed SSC values against predicted SSC values. A good model produces overlapping distributions. A gap at high concentrations means the model is clipping extreme events; a gap at low concentrations means it is over-smoothing baseflow conditions.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-distribution-comparison
from _scripts.figures import make_distribution_comparison

fig = make_distribution_comparison(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Pooled vs. Site-Level Performance

Aggregating all predictions into a single R² number hides a critical problem: a model can look excellent in aggregate while failing badly at specific sites. This figure shows the distribution of R² computed separately for each holdout site. A median site R² well below the pooled R² is a warning sign. Sites with R² below zero are actively worse than predicting the site's long-run average SSC — the model has learned nothing useful for those locations. The spread of this distribution is as important as its center.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-pooled-vs-persite
from _scripts.figures import make_pooled_vs_persite

fig = make_pooled_vs_persite(Path("dashboard_data/per_site.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## CatBoost vs OLS Head-to-Head

The traditional method for converting turbidity to SSC is Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression — a simple log-linear line fit to local samples at each site individually. This comparison asks: does a complex cross-site machine learning model actually beat a simple local regression when both have the same number of local calibration samples? Each point represents one holdout site given 10 local samples (random split from available data). The x-axis shows the OLS R² and the y-axis shows the CatBoost R² at that site. Points above the diagonal mean CatBoost won; points below mean OLS won. At low N, OLS often over-fits to noisy samples, so CatBoost's advantage is expected to be largest here.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-catboost-vs-ols
from _scripts.figures import make_catboost_vs_ols

fig = make_catboost_vs_ols(Path("dashboard_data/ols_ols_benchmark_per_site.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Disaggregated Performance

A single summary metric hides which types of sites or conditions the model handles well vs. poorly. This section breaks model accuracy down by four dimensions: (1) hydrology regime (e.g., rain-dominated vs. snowmelt), (2) how sediment samples were collected in the field (depth-integrated sampling vs. auto-sampler grab vs. manual grab), (3) dominant watershed geology, and (4) the SSC concentration range itself. If a particular subgroup shows sharply lower R², that points to a systematic gap — either a missing feature, a different physical process, or data quality issues in that category.

### Heatmap View

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-disagg-heatmap
from _scripts.figures import make_disaggregated_heatmap

fig = make_disaggregated_heatmap(Path("dashboard_data/diag_disaggregated_metrics.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

### Dot Plot View

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-disagg-dotplot
from _scripts.figures import make_disaggregated_dotplot

fig = make_disaggregated_dotplot(Path("dashboard_data/diag_disaggregated_metrics.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Error Landscape

This figure places every prediction error in context of the true SSC value, revealing how error behavior changes across the full concentration range. At very low SSC (clear water), turbidity sensors respond to dissolved organic matter and algae rather than sediment — the model appears to overpredict because the signal is contaminated, not because the model is wrong. At very high SSC (storm peaks), sediment particle size shifts dramatically; larger particles scatter light differently, causing systematic underprediction. The annotated zones name these physical mechanisms so errors can be interpreted rather than simply counted.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-physics-wall
from _scripts.figures import make_physics_wall

fig = make_physics_wall(Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

## Geology Disaggregation

Different bedrock types produce different sediment compositions — a watershed dominated by soft shales erodes very differently from one underlain by hard granite. This section isolates whether the model's accuracy varies with dominant watershed geology, which would indicate that sediment grain size or mineral composition introduces systematic error that watershed-average features don't fully capture. Two views of the same data are provided; use the one that makes the pattern clearest to you.

<!-- TODO: Verify axis labels and geology category names match the SGMC classification used in the data pipeline — cannot confirm without rendered output. -->

### Grouped Bar View

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-geology-disagg
from _scripts.figures import make_geology_disaggregation

fig = make_geology_disaggregation(Path("dashboard_data/diag_disaggregated_metrics.parquet"))
fig.show()
```

### Slope Graph View

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-geology-slopegraph
from _scripts.figures import make_geology_slopegraph

fig = make_geology_slopegraph(Path("dashboard_data/diag_disaggregated_metrics.parquet"))
fig.show()
```
